unit.name: Neural layers, self-organization, and activation choices
unit.description: Introduces assignment context, revisits feed-forward neural layers, and surveys why differentiable activations replaced threshold units.
unit.summary: The instructor frames assignment 2 as a combined exercise in neural-network math, dependency parsing, and first exposure to PyTorch [ts=50s][ts=87s]. He then revisits multilayer neural networks as cascades of logistic-regression-like units whose hidden layers learn useful intermediate representations for downstream decisions rather than relying only on hand-designed features [ts=243s][ts=285s]. The layer computation is written in vectorized form as z = Wx + b followed by an elementwise activation function f(z) [ts=394s][ts=420s]. He motivates differentiable nonlinearities by contrasting them with threshold units, which have no usable slope for gradient-based learning [ts=515s][ts=550s], and then surveys common choices—sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, leaky ReLU, and newer ReLU-like alternatives—highlighting the trade-off between dead regions and easier gradient flow [ts=590s][ts=690s][ts=813s][ts=847s].

key_points.text: Assignment 2 is meant to build neural-network math intuition while also introducing dependency parsing and PyTorch.
Hidden layers let the network self-organize intermediate representations that are useful for later computations.
A layer is computed as z = Wx + b followed by an elementwise activation function h = f(z).
Threshold activations make learning hard because flat regions provide no gradient.
ReLU became popular because its positive-region slope is always 1, making backward gradient flow simple and effective despite dead negative regions.

He then revisits multilayer neural networks as cascades of logistic-regression-like units whose hidden layers learn useful intermediate representations for downstream decisions rather than relying only on hand-designed features [ts=243s][ts=285s]. The layer computation is written in vectorized form as z = Wx + b followed by an elementwise activation function f(z) [ts=394s][ts=420s]. He motivates differentiable nonlinearities by contrasting them with threshold units, which have no usable slope for gradient-based learning [ts=515s][ts=550s], and then surveys common choices—sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, leaky ReLU, and newer ReLU-like alternatives—highlighting the trade-off between dead regions and easier gradient flow [ts=590s][ts=690s][ts=813s][ts=847s].

kp_catalog.name: Intermediate representation learning
kp_catalog.description: Multilayer neural networks are powerful because hidden layers can learn useful internal features for downstream prediction instead of relying only on manually defined inputs.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because intermediate representation learning formalizes how multilayer neural networks are powerful because hidden layers can learn useful internal features for downstream prediction instead of relying only on manually defined inputs, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Activation functions and nonlinearities
kp_catalog.description: Differentiable nonlinear activation functions such as sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, and ReLU-like variants are needed so networks can model nonlinear functions and support gradient-based learning. The idea that activations between linear maps prevent the network from collapsing into a single linear transformation and allow nonlinear decision boundaries.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because activation functions and nonlinearities formalizes how differentiable nonlinear activation functions such as sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, and ReLU-like variants are needed so networks can model nonlinear functions and support gradient-based learning, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent
kp_catalog.description: Neural networks are trained by computing the gradient of an objective and updating parameters with stochastic gradient descent. Gradient-based optimization updates parameters by subtracting a scaled gradient, with the learning rate controlling step size.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent formalizes how neural networks are trained by computing the gradient of an objective and updating parameters with stochastic gradient descent, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
